title: Drug-Induced, Environmental & Pubertal Gynecomastia: A Case-Based Atlas
subtitle: A Resident Training Module on the 'other' causes beyond the classic anti-androgens — calcium-channel blockers, SSRIs, PPIs and chelators; environmental and dietary endocrine disruptors (lavender/tea-tree oils, soy, bisphenols/phthalates); and pubertal gynecomastia management (PASH, when to operate, and SERM therapy).
authors: Resident training module — synthesis of published cases (see sourceFile); original teaching text. All citations verified/corrected against PubMed/PMC.
sourceFile: user-provided compilation (Gynecomastia_Resident_Training_Module.md), deduplicated against cr-047/048/049 and citations verified/corrected via PubMed/PMC: 'Amlodipine gynaecomastia' Postgrad Med J 2002 PMID 14965638; Kaufman et al. (antidepressant-selective gynecomastia, sertraline) Ann Pharmacother 2013 PMID 23324513; Carvajal et al. (PPI/omeprazole) Drug Saf 2007;30(6):527–531 PMID 17536878; Finer/Emery/Hicks (mammary gigantism & D-penicillamine) Clin Endocrinol (Oxf) 1984;21(3):219–222 PMID 6478628; Henley et al. (lavender & tea-tree oils) N Engl J Med 2007;356:479–485 DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa064725; Martinez & Lewi (soy) Endocr Pract 2008;14:415–418 PMID 18558591; EDC systematic review Front Endocrinol 2026 DOI 10.3389/fendo.2026.1734644 PMC12909171; Saoud et al. (PASH) Ann Med Surg 2024 PMC10798276; tamoxifen for pubertal gynaecomastia Int J Pediatr Adolesc Med 2015 PMC6372400. Duplicate cases from the source (efavirenz, amiodarone, spironolactone/cirrhosis, aging, obesity, uremic, refeeding, PAIS, aromatase excess) are intentionally NOT repeated — they live in cr-047/048/049.

relatedChapters.title: Pubertal Gynecomastia That Is Not Physiological

patient.chiefComplaint: Gynecomastia from a drug, an environmental/dietary exposure, or pubertal physiology — recognised by the exposure history and reversed by dechallenge or time
pmh: Drugs beyond the classic anti-androgens — amlodipine (calcium-channel blocker), sertraline (SSRI), omeprazole (PPI), D-penicillamine (chelator)
Environmental / dietary endocrine disruptors — topical lavender and tea-tree oils, massive soy isoflavone intake, bisphenols/phthalates
Pubertal gynecomastia — persistent/'burnt-out' fibrotic disease, pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH), and medical (SERM) therapy
medications: Implicated agents: amlodipine, sertraline, omeprazole, D-penicillamine; therapeutic SERM (tamoxifen) for pubertal gynecomastia

caseDescription: A case-based atlas of the gynecomastia causes that the headline endocrine syndromes miss: ordinary drugs not usually thought of as anti-androgens (a calcium-channel blocker, an SSRI, a proton-pump inhibitor, a chelator), environmental and dietary endocrine-disrupting chemicals (lavender and tea-tree oils, soy isoflavones, bisphenols and phthalates), and the management of pubertal gynecomastia — including its persistent fibrotic stage, the histologic mimic pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia, and SERM therapy. The common thread is an exposure history that shifts the effective estrogen-to-androgen balance at the breast, usually with a normal hormone panel, and a diagnosis confirmed by dechallenge or by recognising physiology and histology. It is deliberately deduplicated against cr-047 (estrogen excess), cr-048 (androgen deficiency/resistance) and cr-049 (systemic/endocrine/nutritional).
keyFindings: Many everyday drugs cause gynecomastia at a NORMAL serum testosterone — the clue is the exposure timeline, and the test is dechallenge
Environmental/dietary endocrine disruptors (lavender/tea-tree oils, massive soy, bisphenols/phthalates) act as estrogen agonists or androgen antagonists
A massive single exposure (3 quarts/day soy milk) can raise estrone/estradiol several-fold and cause reversible gynecomastia
Pubertal gynecomastia is common (up to ~70% of boys, peak ~14y) and usually resolves; tamoxifen helps before the fibrotic 'burnt-out' stage
PASH (pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia) is a benign, hormone-responsive stromal lesion that can present as male gynecomastia/gigantomastia
finalDiagnosis: These gynecomastias are diagnoses of exposure and physiology, and management is correspondingly conservative. For drug causes (amlodipine, sertraline, omeprazole, D-penicillamine), confirm the temporal link, exclude endocrine/tumour causes with a normal hormone panel, and dechallenge to a safe alternative — most resolve. For environmental/dietary endocrine disruptors, identify and remove the exposure (stop the lavender/tea-tree product, cut the extreme soy intake, reduce bisphenol/phthalate contact) and expect regression. For pubertal gynecomastia, reassure and observe; offer tamoxifen for painful or persistent recent-onset disease before fibrosis sets in, and reserve surgery for long-standing fibrotic or gigantomastic disease — recognising PASH histologically when an excised specimen shows it.
learningObjectives: Recognise gynecomastia from everyday drugs (CCB, SSRI, PPI, chelator) that present with a normal hormone panel, and apply dechallenge logic.
Identify environmental and dietary endocrine disruptors (lavender/tea-tree oils, soy isoflavones, bisphenols/phthalates) and counsel on removal.
Explain how a massive dietary phytoestrogen load can raise estrogens and cause reversible gynecomastia.
Manage pubertal gynecomastia — observation, the role and timing of tamoxifen, and when to operate.
Recognise pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH) as a benign cause/mimic of male gynecomastia and gigantomastia.

items.title: How to think about the 'other' causes — exposure history, a normal hormone panel, and dechallenge
items.content: After the headline causes are considered — estrogen excess (cr-047), androgen deficiency/resistance (cr-048) and systemic/endocrine disease (cr-049) — a large share of real-world gynecomastia comes from ordinary drugs, environmental or dietary exposures, and plain pubertal physiology. These share a diagnostic fingerprint: a NORMAL hormone panel (testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol, prolactin) with the answer sitting in the exposure history rather than the labs. Drugs not usually filed as 'anti-androgens' (a calcium-channel blocker, an SSRI, a proton-pump inhibitor, a chelator) cause gynecomastia idiosyncratically; environmental endocrine-disrupting chemicals act as estrogen agonists or androgen antagonists; and pubertal gynecomastia is a developmental imbalance that mostly self-resolves. The practical method is therefore: confirm true glandular tissue and exclude breast cancer, take a meticulous drug/supplement/cosmetic/diet history, send a basic hormone panel to exclude the dangerous causes, and then dechallenge the suspected exposure — regression confirms it. This module is deduplicated against the other gynecomastia atlases and focuses only on these 'other' causes.
keyPoints: Fingerprint: normal hormone panel + a positive exposure history (drug, environmental, dietary, or pubertal)
Method: confirm glandular tissue, exclude breast cancer, basic hormone panel, then dechallenge
Deduplicated against cr-047/048/049 — only the drug, environmental and pubertal causes here

items.title: Why a calcium-channel blocker belongs on the gynecomastia drug list (Case 1)
items.content: An elderly hypertensive man develops bilateral gynecomastia some months after starting amlodipine, with a normal hormone panel, and it regresses after the drug is stopped or switched ('Amlodipine gynaecomastia', Postgrad Med J 2002; PMID 14965638; the association was also reported earlier in the Medical Journal of Australia, 1994). Calcium-channel blockers — amlodipine, nifedipine, verapamil and diltiazem — are a recognised, if uncommon, cause of gynecomastia; the mechanism is not fully defined but the causality rests on a consistent temporal relationship and resolution on dechallenge. The teaching point is breadth of the drug history: gynecomastia work-ups that stop at spironolactone and the anti-androgens miss the antihypertensive that the patient may not even mention. When a CCB is the plausible culprit and blood pressure can be controlled another way, substitute it and watch for regression rather than escalating to hormonal tests or surgery.
keyPoints: Calcium-channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine, verapamil, diltiazem) are a recognised drug cause
Normal hormone panel; causality from temporal link + dechallenge response
Take a complete drug history — don't stop at the anti-androgens

items.title: How an SSRI can trigger gynecomastia in stable polypharmacy (Case 2)
items.content: A 67-year-old man on a stable regimen of duloxetine, rosuvastatin and amlodipine developed new bilateral gynecomastia and breast tenderness only after sertraline was added, with mammography showing benign bilateral gynecomastia and no suspicious lesion (Kaufman KR et al., 'Antidepressant-selective gynecomastia', Ann Pharmacother 2013; PMID 23324513). The case illustrates 'antidepressant-selective' or synergistic drug-induced gynecomastia: a new agent introduced onto a background of other potentially contributory drugs (here amlodipine) tips the balance, and the breast enlargement is temporally locked to that single addition. SSRIs can raise prolactin modestly and may perturb the estrogen:androgen balance, but the diagnostic engine is again the timeline. The management principle is to identify the specific drug whose addition coincided with onset and, where psychiatrically safe, dechallenge or switch it — distinguishing a true culprit from innocent bystanders in a complex medication list.
keyPoints: SSRIs (e.g. sertraline) can cause gynecomastia, sometimes synergistically in polypharmacy
Onset is time-locked to the newly added drug — that pinpoints the culprit
Dechallenge/switch the implicated agent where psychiatrically safe

items.title: Why proton-pump inhibitors carry a pharmacovigilance signal for gynecomastia (Case 3)
items.content: Proton-pump inhibitors are extremely widely used, so even a low-frequency adverse effect matters at the population level. The Spanish Pharmacovigilance System collected a case series of gynecomastia associated with PPIs, with omeprazole the most frequently implicated (Carvajal A et al., Drug Saf 2007;30(6):527–531; PMID 17536878), complementing earlier single reports of painful gynecomastia during omeprazole therapy (PMID 1682578). Onset ranges from weeks to months and the gynecomastia is typically painful, resolving after the drug is stopped. The mechanism is uncertain (a weak anti-androgenic or prolactin effect has been proposed). The clinical lesson is to ask specifically about over-the-counter and long-term PPI use — patients rarely volunteer a 'stomach tablet' as a medication — and to trial dechallenge before invasive work-up. As always, a hard or eccentric mass still warrants imaging to exclude male breast carcinoma.
keyPoints: PPIs (esp. omeprazole) carry a pharmacovigilance signal for (often painful) gynecomastia
Ask specifically about OTC/long-term PPI use — patients omit 'stomach tablets'
Dechallenge before invasive work-up; image a hard/eccentric mass regardless

items.title: Why D-penicillamine can cause dramatic mammary gigantism (Case 4)
items.content: D-penicillamine — a chelator used in Wilson disease, rheumatoid arthritis and cystinuria — is a rare but striking cause of breast enlargement that can progress to true mammary gigantism (massive, rapid, sometimes grotesque breast overgrowth), described with pathological and endocrine detail and a review of seven prior cases (Finer N, Emery P, Hicks BH, 'Mammary gigantism and D-penicillamine', Clin Endocrinol (Oxf) 1984;21(3):219–222; PMID 6478628). The mechanism is poorly understood and the enlargement is disproportionate to any measurable hormonal change, so it is a reminder that drug-induced breast growth is not always explained by a tidy estrogen:androgen shift. Management has included drug withdrawal, mechanical support and, for refractory gigantism, antiestrogen/danazol therapy or reduction surgery. The teaching value is pattern recognition: when breast enlargement is massive and rapid in a patient on penicillamine, attribute it to the drug, stop it where possible, and involve breast surgery early for gigantomastia.
keyPoints: D-penicillamine (Wilson disease, RA, cystinuria) can cause rapid, massive mammary gigantism
Enlargement is disproportionate to hormonal change — mechanism poorly understood
Withdraw the drug; refractory gigantism may need antiestrogen/danazol or reduction surgery

items.title: How lavender and tea-tree oils cause prepubertal gynecomastia (Case 5)
items.content: Prepubertal boys with repeated topical exposure to products containing lavender oil and/or tea-tree oil developed gynecomastia that regressed after the products were stopped, and laboratory work showed these oils have both estrogenic and anti-androgenic activity in human cell assays (Henley DV, Lipson N, Korach KS, Bloch CA, 'Prepubertal Gynecomastia Linked to Lavender and Tea Tree Oils', N Engl J Med 2007;356:479–485; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa064725). This landmark report established essential oils as endocrine-disrupting chemicals capable of causing clinically apparent gynecomastia, and is the reason prepubertal gynecomastia work-ups must specifically ask about shampoos, lotions, soaps, balms and 'natural'/aromatherapy products. Because prepubertal gynecomastia is never normal, the exposure history sits alongside (not instead of) exclusion of adrenal/testicular tumours and enzyme defects — but a positive lavender/tea-tree history with regression on withdrawal is often the answer.
keyPoints: Lavender and tea-tree oils are estrogenic + anti-androgenic — a proven EDC cause of prepubertal gynecomastia
Ask about shampoos, lotions, balms and 'natural'/aromatherapy products in every prepubertal case
Still exclude tumours/enzyme defects — prepubertal gynecomastia is never normal

items.title: Why a massive soy intake can raise estrogens enough to cause gynecomastia (Case 6)
items.content: A 60-year-old man developed bilateral gynecomastia, erectile dysfunction and decreased libido with estrone and estradiol roughly four-fold above the upper reference limit; the cause was an enormous intake of soy milk — about 3 quarts/day, estimated at ~361 mg of phytoestrogens (mainly the isoflavones genistein and daidzein) — and his estrogens normalised and mastodynia resolved after he stopped the soy (Martinez J, Lewi JE, 'An unusual case of gynecomastia associated with soy product consumption', Endocr Pract 2008;14:415–418; PMID 18558591). The teaching nuance is dose: ordinary dietary soy does not feminise men, but an extreme single-source load can deliver a phytoestrogen dose large enough to shift the estrogen:androgen balance. So a dietary history should quantify unusual or faddish intakes (soy, but also other concentrated phytoestrogen sources), and the diagnosis is confirmed the same way as the drug cases — removal of the exposure followed by biochemical and clinical regression.
keyPoints: Extreme soy intake (~3 quarts/day, ~361 mg isoflavones) raised estrone/estradiol ~4× and caused reversible gynecomastia
It is dose-dependent — ordinary dietary soy does not feminise men
Quantify unusual/faddish dietary intakes; removal → regression confirms it

items.title: How bisphenols and phthalates are linked to gynecomastia — and why the evidence is associative
items.content: Beyond essential oils and soy, ubiquitous synthetic endocrine-disrupting chemicals — bisphenols (e.g. bisphenol A in plastics), phthalates (plasticisers, food packaging, cosmetics) and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons — can mimic estrogen, antagonise the androgen receptor, or interfere with hormone metabolism, and a systematic review collates the experimental and epidemiological signal linking them to male breast development (Front Endocrinol 2026; DOI 10.3389/fendo.2026.1734644; PMC12909171). Bisphenol exposure in particular has been associated with gynecomastia in men. The crucial doubt-flag is the strength of evidence: the review explicitly notes that mechanisms are incompletely defined and that real-world 'mixed exposure' makes causal attribution in an individual patient difficult — so EDCs are a plausible contributory and population-level risk factor rather than a confirmable single-patient diagnosis. Practically, counsel on reducing avoidable exposure (plastics heating/storage, fragranced cosmetics) where an EDC contribution is suspected, but do not over-claim causation in a given man.
keyPoints: Bisphenols, phthalates and PAHs act as estrogen agonists / androgen antagonists
Evidence is associative/experimental — mechanisms unclear and exposures are mixed (doubt-flag)
Counsel on reducing avoidable exposure; avoid over-claiming single-patient causation

items.title: Why most pubertal gynecomastia resolves — and when it becomes a surgical problem
items.content: Pubertal gynecomastia is extremely common — palpable breast tissue affects up to ~70% of adolescent boys with a peak around age 14 — and arises from a transient pubertal estrogen-to-androgen imbalance that, in the large majority, regresses spontaneously within months to a couple of years. The clinical decisions hinge on duration and tissue character. Recent-onset, tender, soft glandular tissue is observed (and may respond to medical therapy). But once gynecomastia has been present beyond roughly 12 months it tends to fibrose and hyalinise into a 'burnt-out' stage that no longer responds to hormonal change, and persistent disease at the end of puberty — particularly large or gigantomastic forms — is a surgical problem (subcutaneous mastectomy ± liposuction, or reduction mammoplasty for gigantomastia). The practical rule mirrors the rest of gynecomastia care: treat early while the tissue is still glandular and reversible, and reserve surgery for established fibrotic or gigantomastic disease after puberty completes.
keyPoints: Pubertal gynecomastia affects up to ~70% of boys (peak ~14y) and usually self-resolves
Beyond ~12 months it fibroses ('burnt-out') and stops responding to hormonal change
Surgery is for persistent post-pubertal, fibrotic or gigantomastic disease

items.title: How pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH) presents as male gynecomastia (Case — histologic mimic)
items.content: Pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH) is a benign proliferation of mammary myofibroblastic stroma that forms slit-like, empty pseudovascular spaces lined by spindle cells (CD34-positive, hormone-receptor-responsive, NOT true vascular channels). In men it can present as gynecomastia or, occasionally, as rapid gigantomastia in adolescents, and it is frequently found incidentally within gynecomastia specimens (Saoud S et al., 'Pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia: a rare cause of gynecomastia in men', Ann Med Surg 2024; PMC10798276, reporting a 17-year-old with breast enlargement and a mild recurrence at 8 months). The key points are that PASH is benign and hormone-responsive (so it overlaps clinically with ordinary gynecomastia), that its 'angioma-like' spaces on histology must not be mistaken for angiosarcoma (the pseudovascular spaces are empty and CD34+/factor VIII-negative), and that management is conservative excision with follow-up because it can recur. Recognising PASH explains some cases of otherwise 'idiopathic' or rapidly enlarging male breast tissue.
keyPoints: PASH = benign myofibroblastic stromal proliferation with slit-like CD34+ pseudovascular spaces (not true vessels)
Presents as male gynecomastia or adolescent gigantomastia; often incidental in gynecomastia specimens
Don't mistake it for angiosarcoma; treat with conservative excision and follow-up (can recur)

items.title: How tamoxifen is used to treat pubertal gynecomastia, and why timing matters (Case 9)
items.content: Watchful waiting and surgery are the traditional options for pubertal gynecomastia, but a selective estrogen-receptor modulator offers a medical middle path: tamoxifen 20 mg/day for about six months was reported as safe, well tolerated and effective for male pubertal gynaecomastia, allowing a patient to avoid surgery, with surgery reserved for gynecomastia persisting at the end of puberty after a tamoxifen trial fails ('Tamoxifen to treat male pubertal gynaecomastia', Int J Pediatr Adolesc Med 2015; PMC6372400). The mechanistic rationale is that tamoxifen blocks estrogen action at the breast, and it works best while the tissue is still glandular and hormonally responsive — which is exactly why timing matters: a SERM offered early (painful, recent-onset disease) has a good response rate, whereas long-standing fibrotic 'burnt-out' tissue will not regress and needs surgery. Tamoxifen is generally used off-label for this indication; counsel accordingly and monitor.
keyPoints: Tamoxifen 20 mg/day × ~6 months is an effective medical option for pubertal gynecomastia
Works while tissue is glandular/responsive — offer early; fibrotic disease needs surgery
Off-label use — counsel and monitor; reserve surgery for tamoxifen failure at end of puberty

items.title: How to work up and act on a drug/environmental/pubertal cause — and the cases at a glance
items.content: Workflow: confirm true glandular tissue and exclude male breast cancer (image a hard/eccentric/fixed mass or bloody discharge); take an exhaustive exposure history (every prescription and OTC drug, supplements, cosmetics/essential oils, diet, and pubertal timeline); send a basic hormone panel (testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol, prolactin ± TSH, hCG) to exclude the dangerous causes — it is typically normal in these aetiologies; then dechallenge the suspected exposure and expect regression. Cases at a glance — Drugs: amlodipine/CCB (Case 1, switch antihypertensive), sertraline/SSRI (Case 2, time-locked to the added drug), omeprazole/PPI (Case 3, ask about OTC use), D-penicillamine (Case 4, can cause gigantism). Environmental/dietary: lavender & tea-tree oils (Case 5, prepubertal, stop the product), massive soy (Case 6, dose-dependent), bisphenols/phthalates (associative — counsel on exposure). Pubertal: most resolve and tamoxifen helps early (Cases on persistence & SERM), surgery for fibrotic/gigantomastic disease, and recognise PASH on histology.
keyPoints: Confirm glandular tissue → exclude cancer → exhaustive exposure history → basic (usually normal) panel → dechallenge
Drugs: CCB, SSRI, PPI, D-penicillamine. Environmental: lavender/tea-tree, soy, bisphenols/phthalates
Pubertal: observe/tamoxifen early; surgery for fibrotic/gigantomastic; PASH is a histologic finding

items.title: Module appraisal — strengths, limitations and citation notes
items.content: Strength: this atlas captures the high-frequency, easily-missed real-world causes (everyday drugs, environmental/dietary exposures, pubertal physiology) that the syndrome-focused atlases under-emphasise, and it is explicitly deduplicated against cr-047/048/049 so the cluster does not repeat itself. Citation note: every case was checked against PubMed/PMC; where the source compilation's author lists were unreliable they were trimmed to verified records (e.g. the antidepressant case is Kaufman et al., Ann Pharmacother 2013 — the source's co-author names were garbled; the tamoxifen paper is in the Int J Pediatr Adolesc Med, not 'Int J Pediatr Endocrinol'; the amlodipine report's authorship could not be independently confirmed so it is cited by title/journal/PMID). Doubt-flags: the bisphenol/phthalate link rests on a systematic review of partial experimental and epidemiological evidence with incompletely defined mechanisms and mixed real-world exposures, so it is a population-level/contributory association, not a confirmable single-patient diagnosis; and several drug associations (CCB, SSRI, PPI) are individually uncommon and rest on case reports and pharmacovigilance signals rather than controlled data. Lab values and doses are illustrative; tamoxifen for pubertal gynecomastia is off-label. Statements are educational, not prescriptive.
keyPoints: Citations verified/corrected: Kaufman et al. (SSRI); Int J Pediatr Adolesc Med (tamoxifen); amlodipine cited by title/PMID (author unconfirmed)
Bisphenol/phthalate evidence is associative (systematic review, mixed exposures) — not single-patient proof
Drug associations rest on case reports/pharmacovigilance; tamoxifen use is off-label; statements are educational

items.question: Why is a careful exposure history more useful than the hormone panel for diagnosing drug-, diet- and environment-related gynecomastia?
items.answer: Because these causes characteristically present with a NORMAL hormone panel — testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol and prolactin are usually unremarkable — so the labs mainly serve to exclude the dangerous endocrine and tumoural causes rather than to make the diagnosis. The diagnostic signal lives in the timeline and the exposure: gynecomastia that began weeks to months after a new drug (amlodipine, sertraline, omeprazole, D-penicillamine), after starting a lavender/tea-tree product, or during an extreme dietary intake (soy). Patients frequently omit the relevant exposure unless asked specifically — they don't think of a 'stomach tablet', an antihypertensive, a shampoo, or a health-food habit as relevant. The confirmation is pharmacovigilance logic: dechallenge (stop/substitute the exposure) produces regression, and in the strongest cases rechallenge reproduces it. So the history finds the cause and the dechallenge proves it, while the hormone panel's job is to rule out the things you must not miss.
keyPoints: These causes show a normal hormone panel — labs exclude dangerous causes, they don't make the diagnosis
The timeline + a specifically-elicited exposure history is the diagnostic engine
Dechallenge (± rechallenge) confirms causality

items.question: How should the timing of pubertal gynecomastia guide the choice between observation, tamoxifen, and surgery?
items.answer: Map the intervention to the tissue stage. Early, recent-onset pubertal gynecomastia (tender, soft, glandular) is best observed, because up to ~70% of boys have it and most regress within months to ~2 years. If it is painful, distressing, or not regressing but still glandular and within roughly the first 12 months, a SERM — tamoxifen ~20 mg/day for about six months — is an effective, well-tolerated medical option that can avoid surgery, because the tissue is still estrogen-responsive. Once gynecomastia has persisted beyond ~12 months it fibroses and hyalinises into a 'burnt-out' stage that no longer responds to hormonal manipulation, so persistent disease at the end of puberty — and large or gigantomastic forms — is treated surgically (subcutaneous mastectomy ± liposuction, or reduction mammoplasty). In short: observe early, treat medically while glandular, and operate for fibrotic or gigantomastic disease after puberty completes.
keyPoints: Observe recent-onset glandular disease (most resolve)
Tamoxifen while still glandular/responsive (≈first 12 months), to avoid surgery
Surgery for fibrotic 'burnt-out' or gigantomastic disease at end of puberty

items.stem: An elderly hypertensive man develops bilateral gynecomastia 4 months after starting amlodipine, with a normal testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol and prolactin. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Substitute the calcium-channel blocker with another antihypertensive and observe for regression
items.explanation: Calcium-channel blockers are a recognised drug cause of gynecomastia; with a normal hormone panel and a clear temporal link, the diagnosis is drug-induced and the move is dechallenge — substitute the CCB and watch for regression. Surgery is premature, testosterone is not indicated, and a pituitary MRI is unwarranted with a normal prolactin and no other features.

items.stem: A 67-year-old man on stable duloxetine, rosuvastatin and amlodipine develops gynecomastia within weeks of ADDING sertraline. Which feature most strongly implicates sertraline?
options: The onset is time-locked to the single newly added drug
items.explanation: In polypharmacy, the drug whose introduction coincides temporally with the onset is the most likely culprit — here sertraline, added onto a stable regimen ('antidepressant-selective' gynecomastia). Bilaterality, age, and a glandular mammogram are consistent with gynecomastia but do not single out the causative agent.

items.stem: Which medication history is easily missed but carries a pharmacovigilance signal for painful gynecomastia?
options: Long-term or over-the-counter omeprazole (a proton-pump inhibitor)
items.explanation: Proton-pump inhibitors, especially omeprazole, carry a pharmacovigilance signal for (often painful) gynecomastia, and patients frequently fail to mention an OTC or long-term 'stomach tablet'. The other agents are not associated with gynecomastia.

items.stem: A patient on D-penicillamine develops rapid, massive breast enlargement out of proportion to any hormonal change. This is best described as:
options: Mammary gigantism, a recognised complication of D-penicillamine
An allergic drug reaction unrelated to the breast
items.explanation: D-penicillamine is a rare but striking cause of mammary gigantism — rapid, massive breast overgrowth disproportionate to measurable hormonal change. It is benign but often refractory; management includes drug withdrawal and, for refractory gigantism, antiestrogen/danazol or reduction surgery. It is not physiological, not carcinoma, and not a simple allergy.

items.stem: A prepubertal boy develops gynecomastia that regresses after stopping a fragranced balm. Which exposure is the established endocrine-disrupting cause?
items.explanation: Lavender and tea-tree oils have estrogenic and anti-androgenic activity and are an established cause of prepubertal gynecomastia that regresses on withdrawal (Henley et al., NEJM 2007). The other exposures are not recognised causes. (Prepubertal gynecomastia still mandates exclusion of tumours/enzyme defects.)

items.stem: A 60-year-old man has gynecomastia with estrone and estradiol four-fold above normal. He drinks about 3 quarts of soy milk daily. What is the most likely explanation and management?
options: Phytoestrogen (isoflavone) excess from massive soy intake — stop the soy and expect regression
An estrogen-secreting testicular tumour — proceed to orchidectomy
Normal dietary soy intake, unrelated to the gynecomastia
Klinefelter syndrome — obtain a karyotype first
items.explanation: An extreme soy load (~3 quarts/day, ~361 mg isoflavones) can raise estrogens several-fold and cause reversible gynecomastia; estrogens normalise and symptoms resolve after stopping the soy. Ordinary dietary soy does not feminise men, so this is dose-dependent; the markedly elevated estrogens with a clear dietary source make a tumour or Klinefelter unlikely as the primary explanation.

items.stem: How should the link between bisphenols/phthalates and gynecomastia be characterised when counselling a patient?
options: An associative, population-level/contributory risk with incompletely defined mechanisms, not a confirmable single-patient diagnosis
A proven, quantifiable cause in every exposed individual
Diagnosable by a specific serum bisphenol level
items.explanation: The evidence (a systematic review of partial experimental and epidemiological data) supports bisphenols/phthalates as estrogenic/anti-androgenic endocrine disruptors associated with male breast development at a population level, but mechanisms are unclear and real-world exposures are mixed — so it is a contributory/associative risk, not a confirmable single-patient diagnosis, and there is no diagnostic serum level. Counsel on reducing avoidable exposure without over-claiming causation.

items.stem: A 15-year-old has painful gynecomastia present for 4 months that is still soft and glandular. After reassurance, which medical option is best supported?
items.explanation: While the tissue is still glandular and recent-onset, tamoxifen (~20 mg/day for ~6 months) is a safe, well-tolerated and effective SERM option that can avoid surgery. Mastectomy is reserved for persistent/fibrotic or gigantomastic disease at the end of puberty; testosterone is not indicated; a PPI can itself cause gynecomastia.

items.stem: A male gynecomastia specimen shows slit-like, empty pseudovascular spaces lined by CD34-positive spindle cells. What is the diagnosis, and what is the key pitfall?
options: Pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH) — do not mistake it for angiosarcoma
Invasive ductal carcinoma — perform mastectomy and axillary dissection
items.explanation: These are the histologic hallmarks of pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH): benign myofibroblastic proliferation with empty, slit-like CD34-positive pseudovascular spaces. The crucial pitfall is mistaking it for angiosarcoma — but the spaces are empty and CD34-positive/factor VIII-negative, and PASH is benign (conservative excision, can recur). It is not carcinoma or normal tissue.

items.statement: Most drug-induced gynecomastia (e.g. from a calcium-channel blocker, SSRI or PPI) presents with a clearly abnormal hormone panel.
items.explanation: False. These drug causes characteristically present with a NORMAL hormone panel; the diagnosis rests on the exposure timeline and dechallenge response, while the labs serve mainly to exclude the dangerous endocrine and tumoural causes.

items.statement: Ordinary dietary soy intake reliably feminises men and causes gynecomastia.
items.explanation: False. Gynecomastia from soy is dose-dependent and requires an extreme intake (the reported case drank ~3 quarts of soy milk daily, ~361 mg isoflavones); ordinary dietary soy does not feminise men.

items.statement: Pubertal gynecomastia that has been present for well over a year and become firm/fibrotic generally responds well to tamoxifen.
items.explanation: False. Once gynecomastia fibroses into the 'burnt-out' stage (typically beyond ~12 months) it no longer responds to hormonal manipulation, including a SERM; tamoxifen works while the tissue is still glandular and estrogen-responsive, and surgery is required for established fibrotic disease.

items.assertion: Repeated use of lavender- or tea-tree-oil products can cause prepubertal gynecomastia.
items.reason: These essential oils have both estrogenic and anti-androgenic activity in human cell-based assays.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion: lavender and tea-tree oils act as endocrine disruptors (estrogenic + anti-androgenic), which is how repeated topical exposure produces gynecomastia that regresses on withdrawal (Henley et al., NEJM 2007). Hence option 0.

items.assertion: When gynecomastia follows the addition of a single new drug in a complex regimen, rechallenge is generally required to make any management decision.
items.reason: Dechallenge — stopping or substituting the suspected drug and observing regression — is usually sufficient to act on a drug cause.
items.explanation: The assertion is false: you do not need rechallenge (which risks re-inducing the problem) to act — dechallenge with observed regression is usually enough and is the standard approach. The reason is true. A false assertion with a true reason is option 3.

items.question: Name four drugs covered in this module — beyond the classic anti-androgens — that can cause gynecomastia, and the single diagnostic step that links them.
items.answer: Amlodipine (a calcium-channel blocker), sertraline (an SSRI), omeprazole (a proton-pump inhibitor), and D-penicillamine (a chelator, which can cause mammary gigantism). The linking diagnostic step is a thorough exposure/medication history identifying a temporal relationship, followed by dechallenge (stopping or substituting the drug) with observed regression — all typically occur with a normal hormone panel.

items.question: List the environmental/dietary endocrine disruptors in this module and how each is managed.
items.answer: Topical lavender and tea-tree oils (estrogenic/anti-androgenic — stop the product; prepubertal gynecomastia regresses); massive soy/isoflavone intake (dose-dependent phytoestrogen excess — reduce the extreme intake; estrogens normalise); and bisphenols/phthalates (estrogen-agonist/androgen-antagonist EDCs — counsel on reducing avoidable exposure, recognising the evidence is associative rather than single-patient proof).

items.question: What is PASH, and what is the one histologic pitfall to avoid?
items.answer: Pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH) is a benign, hormone-responsive proliferation of mammary myofibroblastic stroma that forms slit-like, empty pseudovascular spaces lined by CD34-positive spindle cells; in men it can present as gynecomastia or adolescent gigantomastia and is often incidental in gynecomastia specimens. The pitfall is mistaking its 'angioma-like' pseudovascular spaces for angiosarcoma — but in PASH the spaces are empty and CD34-positive/factor VIII-negative, and the lesion is benign (conservative excision, can recur).
